coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for highly sensitive determination of cobalt ions and biological imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Min
Zhang, Junqiu
Liu, Yaoming
Wang, Yingte
Zhang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile, economical and green method for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was established via hydrothermal synthesis using p-phenylenediamine and asparagine. The as-prepared N-CDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD), Raman, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and other methods. Furthermore, the N-CDs showed high sensitivity toward cobalt (II) ion (Co2+). Interestingly, the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched linearly in the 0.3–65 μM Co2+ concentrations range, under the optimum conditions, displaying a fast response toward Co2+ with the detection limit as low as 22 nM. Simultaneously, this method was applied in the analysis of water samples with considerable results. Finally, the N-CDs were successfully utilized as the imaging probe of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana, demonstrating that the N-CDs possessed significant theoretical research meaning in the fields of biological correlation analysis, environmental monitoring and disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Determination
Cobalt ions
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Co2+ in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from the Shanxi Natural Science Foundation (201801D121074).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (b) Size distribution (c) XRD (d) Raman of the N-CDs.
(a) FT- IR (b) XPS spectra and high-resolution XPS of (c) C1s (d) N1s and (e) O1s for N-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption and FL spectra (b) Excitation-emission matrix (c) CIE coordinates of the as-prepared N-CDs.
Effect of (a) pH (b) ionic strength (c) storage time on the FL intensity of the N-CDs aqueous (d) Variation in the FL intensity with reaction time after Co2+ added.
FL responses of N-CDs towards (a) 20 μL different metal ions (b) 20 μL Co2+ and 60 μL coexisting metal ions.
(a) FL emission spectra of the N-CDs after different concentrations of Co2+ added (b) Linear relationship between the concentration of Co2+ and F 0/F.
(a) FL decay of N-CDs without/with Co2+ (b) Overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of Co2+ and the FL emission spectrum of N-CDs.

(A) Confocal fluorescence imaging of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana incubated without N-CDs as a control and with N-CDs (B) The changes of FL signal with the additional of Co2+ in biological imaging of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana over time.
Preparation of N-CDs and its application.
Comparison of optical properties, QY and application of the prepared N-CDs with the published N-CDs.

row.entry: in vivo/in vitro imaging and fluorescent ink
Fe3+ sensing, cell imaging and fluorescent ink

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different methods for Co(II) detection.
Determination of Co2+ in actual water sample (n = 5).

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for highly sensitive determination of cobalt ions and biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Clean and Efficient Coal Utilization, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Clean and Efficient Coal Utilization, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Coal Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Coal Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Coal Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel green-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) had been successfully prepared using p-phenylenediamine and asparagine as precursors by one step hydrothermal-assisted method. The proposed N-CDs, which held extraordinary water dispersibility, high photostability and remarkable biocompatibility, could selectivity and specificity determine Co2+ based on the inner filter effect. Interestingly, the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched linearly in the 0.3–65 μM Co2+ concentrations range, under the optimum conditions, displaying a fast response toward Co2+ with the detection limit of 22 nM. Furthermore, this method was applied to analyze the content of Co2+ in actual samples with satisfying results. More importantly, the N-CDs were utilized as the imaging probe of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana, which effectively overcame the short-wavelength emission of N-CDs analysis technology defects and possessed significant theoretical research meaning in the fields of biological correlation analysis, environmental monitoring and disease diagnosis.

In this work, a facile, economical and green method for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was established via hydrothermal synthesis using p-phenylenediamine and asparagine. The as-prepared N-CDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD), Raman, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and other methods. Furthermore, the N-CDs showed high sensitivity toward cobalt (II) ion (Co2+). Interestingly, the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched linearly in the 0.3–65 μM Co2+ concentrations range, under the optimum conditions, displaying a fast response toward Co2+ with the detection limit as low as 22 nM. Simultaneously, this method was applied in the analysis of water samples with considerable results. Finally, the N-CDs were successfully utilized as the imaging probe of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana, demonstrating that the N-CDs possessed significant theoretical research meaning in the fields of biological correlation analysis, environmental monitoring and disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Co2+ is a consequential element in the human body, playing a physiological role in the form of vitamin B12. However, excessive Co2+ can lead to toxic reactions and the intake of excessive cobalt salts can also cause adverse effects related to myocarditis, gastrointestinal dysfunction, deafness and weakened ability of the thyroid to absorb iodine [1–4]. At present, existing approaches for the determination of Co2+, such as liquid chromatography, electrochemical and atomic absorption spectrophotometry method [5–8], have been reported. Unfortunately, above analytical techniques are restricted by the relatively sophisticated construction procedure, expensive testing and complicated sample treatment. For these reasons, it is of vital importance to establish a reliable approach to detect low concentrations of Co2+.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which are a promising fluorescent nanomaterial with size less than 10 nm, possess environment-friendly, high photostability and remarkable biocompatibility, drawing widespread attention in multiplexed sensing, drug release, disease diagnosis and bioimaging fields [9–17]. The optical properties of CDs are found with a close dependency on the type of precursor, reaction medium and synthesis conditions. Currently, the luminescence performance of CDs can be effectively improved by heteroatom doping or surface modification. However, most of them possess low quantum yields (QY). The emission wavelength is relatively short, mainly concentrated in the blue and green regions, strictly hampering its further applications in biology-relevant analysis due to the blue auto-fluorescence interference and light scattering effect [18–20]. For example, Miao et al [21] prepared CDs adopting citric acid and thiourea in N, N-dimethylformide solution, but QY of the CDs was only 8% in the red light emission. Hu et al [22] synthesized red fluorescence CDs through complicated procedures, with the QY only 6%. Consequently, developing a facile method to prepare long-wavelength emissive CDs with high QY are imperative.

In this work, green-emitting N-CDs displayed in Scheme 1 are fabricated adopting p-phenylenediamine and asparagine as precursor via hydrothermal method. The N-CDs hold excitation-dependent multiple color emission, high photostability and robust chemical inertness. The fluorescence of N-CDs can be selectively quenched by Co2+ through inner filter effct, under the optimum conditions, displaying a fast response toward Co2+ with the detection limit of 22 nM. Furthermore, this method is applied to analyze the content of Co2+ in actual samples with considerable results. More importantly, the N-CDs are utilized as the imaging probe of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana, which effectively overcomes the short-wavelength emission of N-CDs analysis technology defects and possesses significant theoretical research meaning in the fields of biological correlation analysis, environmental monitoring and disease diagnosis.

0.4336 g of p-phenylenediamine was introduced into 20 mL of ethanol with dissolving 0.3982 g of asparagine and continuously stirred until a homogeneous solution formed. The resulting solution was then poured into the high-pressure autoclave (100 mL) and kept in the oven (200 ℃, 8 h). Subsequently, the resulting N-CDs solution, centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10 mins, was purified with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The brown N-CDs powder was eventually obtained by drying.

The QY of N-CDs was calculated by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of N-CDs solution with Rhodamine 6 G (excitation wavelength: 488 nm, quantum yield 0.94, dissolved in ethanol). In order to minimize re-absorption effect, the absorbance of the N-CDs solution was kept below 0.05. The QY was measured based on the following equation:
QC = QR * IC/IR * AR/AC * (nC/nR)2
where Q is the QY, I refers to the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance at excitation wavelength, and n represents the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “R” and “C” stand for standard with known QY and the sample, respectively.

Following these steps for fluorescence sensing: Firstly, 30 μL of N-CDs aqueous solution (CN-CDs = 1.3 mg/mL) was gradually introduced to 2 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.0), followed by individually adding a series of known contents of Co2+ solution. The fluorescence spectra were then recorded under a λmax values of 465 nm. Eventually, the linear range and detection limit, based on the change in fluorescence intensity at 545 nm, were calculated. Similarly, in the selectivity experiment, the other metal cations instead of Co2+ were added into the test system, and recorded the change of F0/F to evaluate the selectivity of N-CDs towards Co2+. (λex/λem = 465 nm/545 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Co2+ in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of established method, water samples were performed for Co2+ determination. First, the water samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove large particles. Then, the various known amounts of Co2+ were individually added into the water samples. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were performed as described in Section 2.3.

The morphological structure of N-CDs was studied by TEM. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, b, N-CDs were quasi-spherical shape and possessed extraordinary dispersibility, with the average particle sizes of 3.15 nm. Meanwhile, the inset of Fig. 1a (HRTEM image) shown that the lattice spacing of N-CDs was 0.22 nm. The XRD pattern shown in Fig. 1c depicted an intense diffraction peak centered at 20.7°, representing to the C(001) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials. And the weak diffraction peak appeared about 42° belong to the C(100) plane.[23,24] The Raman spectrum in Fig. 1d exhibited two characteristic peaks at 1363 cm−1 (disordered D-band) and 1556 cm−1 (crystalline G-band)[24]. And calculated intensity ratio of ID/IG was 0.88, revealing a graphite-like structure.

The surface functional groups and chemical composition of the N-CDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS spectra, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of the N-CDs (Fig. 2 a) had the absorption peaks of NH and OH (3342 cm−1), ArH (3021 cm−1), CO (1709 cm−1), CN (1172 cm−1) [25] and CC (1515 cm−1) [26]. Besides, it could be seen from the XPS spectrum that there were three characteristic peaks of C1s (285.4 eV), N1s (398.9 eV), O1s (532.1 eV), and the C, N and O contents were 70.03%, 17.93% and 12.03%, respectively (Fig. 2b). Among them, the C1s spectrum contained three peaks of 284.4 eV, 285.3 eV and 288.4 eV, assigned to CC, CN, and CO, respectively [27] (Fig. 2c). The peaks at about 399.0 eV, 400.0 eV in the N1s spectrum belong to CN and NH, respectively [28] (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution O1s spectrum included two peaks: 531.4 eV, 532.6 eV, indicating that COH and CO are present [26,29] (Fig. 2e). The above results demonstrated that N-CDs contained aromatic structures, and the structures of nitrogen and oxygen related functional groups were doped in the N-CDs system, improving the water solubility of N-CDs.

To understand the optical performances of the N-CDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were examined. As seen in Fig. 3 a, the characteristic absorption peak of the N-CDs appeared at 242 nm, assigned to the π-π* transitions (CC) [30,31,32]. Moreover, the excitation/emission peaks were at 465/545 nm, alone with the bright green fluorescence. The different emission spectra of the N-CDs were measured under a series of excitation wavelengths (420 nm-520 nm). And the results in Fig. 3b illustrated that N-CDs held the excitation-dependent optical behaviour and the optimal emission wavelength was located at 545 nm, where the optical phenomenon may be associated with the size variation and the distinct emissive trap sites [33–37]. Meanwhile, the CIE diagram (CIE coordinates: 0.3183, 0.627) also exhibited the luminescence properties of N-CDs, visualized in Fig. 3c, proving the application prospects of N-CDs as a fluorescence biosensing platform. Furthermore, the QY of N-CDs was calculated to be 15.5% (Rhodamine B as a reference). And the comparison of the optical properties, QY and applications of the N-CDs designed from different carbon sources was shown in Table 1
[38–44]. The results indicated that the N-CDs prepared adopting p-phenylenediamine and asparagine possessed comparable optical characteristics and high QY compared to the present other N-CDs.

To analyse the detection of Co2+ by N-CDs as fluorescent probes, some analytical parameters (pH, ionic strength, storage time) were optimized. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at different pH solution was presented in Fig. 4 a. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was stable and can detect Co2+ with high sensitivity under pH = 7. So, pH = 7 was the optimal pH value. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs with NaCl introduced (0–0.826 M) kept stable, manifesting that the N-CDs possessed wonderful durability at higher ionic strength (Fig. 4b). We can see from the Fig. 4c that N-CDs were well in a homogeneous phase and remained stable with storing N-CDs in the refrigerator for one month. The results in Fig. 4d shown that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs adding Co2+ decreased rapidly after 2 mins of reaction and had durability over time, so 2 mins was chosen as the action time of N-CDs and Co2+.

The selectivity and sensing capability were a vital issue to assess their applications of N-CDs as fluorescent sensor. When fourteen representative metal ions were added to the N-CDs solution (VN-CDs = 2 mL, CN-CDs = 1.3 mg/mL) , including Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+ (Cmetal ions = 0.01 μΜ), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was only quenched selectively by Co2+ among all common metal ions, suggesting that N-CDs held specific response to Co2+ (Fig. 5 a). It was generally believed that Co2+ could interact with specific functional groups existed on the surface of N-CDs and then enhance the identify Co2+ specifically, giving rise to fluorescence quenching.

Control experiments were also studied to determine whether the coexisting metal ions had interference on the fluorescence response of N-CDs toward Co2+. The fluorescence signal of N-CDs with the coexistent metal ions added, seen in Fig. 5b, was nearly conform with that of pure N-CDs, whereas the fluorescence intensity was resulted in significant decline in the presence of Co2+, which explained that N-CDs were capable to recognize Co2+ with high selectivity and strong anti-interference ability toward all coexisting metal ions.

The sensing process of the present probe was carried out over a wide Co2+ range. As exhibited in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs around 545 nm was homogenously quenched as the Co2+ concentration increased from 0 to 150 μΜ, alone with the quenching efficiency of 96%. Furthermore, the detection of Co2+ performed well in the range of 0.3–65 μM, and the linear regression equation was F 0/F = 0.082C(Co 2+ ) + 0.7577 (R 2 = 0.997; here, F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensities at 545 nm in the absence/presence of Co2+, respectively. (Fig. 6b). A 22 nM limit of detection, calculated by LOD = 3δ/s, was obtained. It was well below the maximum Co2+ content allowed in drinking water (680 nM) and the analytical results had been compared with that of the present probe as shown in Table 2
[45–49]. Clearly, the value of LOD of the established chemosensor was lower than that of recently published Co2+ probes, elucidating that the proposed sensor was appropriate for the selective and sensitive determination of Co2+.

The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism was analyzed by fluorescence lifetime curve, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. In Fig. 7 a, it could be found that the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs only slightly attenuated from 5.05 ns to 5.04 ns with the introduction of Co2+, having no significant change, which shown that there was no significant electron transfer during fluorescence quenching. Besides, we also observed that the optimal emission wavelength of N-CDs was 545 nm, and the absorption peak of Co2+ appeared at 510 nm overlaped with the emission spectrum of N-CDs, implying that Co2+ can shield the emission light of N-CDs and lead to their fluorescence quenching (Fig. 7b). The analytical results indicated that Co2+-induced fluorescence quenching stemed from inner filter effect, conformed with literature reports [50,51].

To investigate the practicality of established probe, two water samples were performed for Co2+ detection (known: no Co2+ was determined in the sample, and the standard concentrations of Co2+ added to the samples were 5.0 μM, 10.0 μM, and 15.0 μM, respectively). The results in Table 3 shown that the corresponding recovery range was 97.80–103.70% and the relative standard deviations were all kept below 3% with satisfying results.

Due to the widespread pollution of Co2+ in agriculture, some plants can enrich higher Co2+ concentrations in their plant tissues, evoking critical phytotoxicity and even impairinglant death. So, we chose the live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana as a model to study if N-CDs could identify the presence of Co2+. In Fig. 8 A(d-f), it could be seen that the morphology of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana was well maintained and the green fluorescent N-CDs was also well dispersed under excited with a 488 nm laser, implying that the N-CDs had been penetrated the cell wall and cell membrane based on its less toxicity, small size and excellent biocompatibility (live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana incubated without N-CDs as a control. Fig. 8A(a-c)). Furthermore, with the addition of Co2+, the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs gradually decreased over time. (Fig. 8B). It can be concluded from the results that the N-CDs can be employed as the imaging probe of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana and effectively detect Co2+ in their plant tissues, proving the significant application potential of N-CDs in the vitro bioimaging.

Green-emitting N-CDs were conveniently prepared using p-phenylenediamine and asparagine by a facile hydrothermal-assisted strategy. The prepared N-CDs possessed small size, low toxicity, favorable photostability and excitation-dependent multiple color emission, empowering N-CDs with great potential to multiplexed sensing, drug release, disease diagnosis and bioimaging. Besides, Co2+ led to fluorescence quenching of N-CDs from inner filter effect, under the optimum conditions, displaying a fast response toward Co2+ with the detection limit of 22 nM. Meanwhile, a method for specififically sensing Co2+ was constructed and smoothly utilized for the analysis of actual samples. More importantly, the N-CDs were applied for biological imaging of live root tip tissue of Arabidopsis thaliana owing to its less toxicity, small size and superior biocompatibility. It is expected that the established fluorescence sensing method could be used for the high-sensitivity detection of Co2+ in clinical diagnosis and environmental analysis, and produce significant theoretical research meaning.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from the Shanxi Natural Science Foundation (201801D121074).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119541.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioaccumulation of cobalt in silkworm (Bombyxmori L.) in relation to mulberry, soil and wastewater metal concentrations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ashfaq, S. Ali, M. A. Hanif. Bioaccumulation of cobalt in silkworm (Bombyxmori L.) in relation to mulberry, soil and wastewater metal concentrations. Process Biochem. 44 (10) (2009) 1179-1187.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cobalt metabolism and toxicology—A brief update

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. O. Simonsen, H. Harbak, P. Bennekou. Cobalt metabolism and toxicology—A brief update. Sci Total Environ. 432 (2012) 210-215.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric detection andremoval of radioactive Co ions using sodium alginate-based composite beads

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Kim, A. Jo, H. M. Yang, B. K. Seo, K. W. Lee, T. S. Lee. Colorimetric detection andremoval of radioactive Co ions using sodium alginate-based composite beads. J Hazard Mater. 326 (2017) 69-76.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), Toxicological Profile for Cobalt, Public Health Service, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services. Talanta. GA. 1992.

Determination of iron, cobalt, nickel, manganese, zinc, copper, cadmium and lead in human hair by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry. Spectrochim Acta B. 57B(8) (2002) 1333-1338.

Sep Sci Technol. 52(1) (2017) 81-90.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Maity, R. Gupta, R. Gunupuru, D. N. Srivastava, P Paul. Calix[4] arene functionalized gold nanoparticles: Application in colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of cobalt ion in organic and aqueous medium. Sensor Actuat B Chem. 191 (2014) 757-764.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Q. Ding, A. Zhu, Y. Tian. Functional Surface Engineering of C-Dots for Fluorescent Biosensing and in Vivo Bioimaging. Accounts Chem Res. 47(1) (2014) 20-30.
